solution: Add *.serialconsole.azure.com to firewall allow list. For US Gov cloud, use *.serialconsole.azure.us.

symptom: Azure Serial Console does not work with storage account using Azure Data Lake Storage Gen2 with hierarchical namespaces.
rootCause: Serial Console is incompatible with ADLS Gen2 hierarchical namespace storage accounts for boot diagnostics.
solution: Create a separate boot diagnostics storage account without ADLS Gen2/hierarchical namespaces. This setting can only be set at creation time.

symptom: Azure Serial Console connects but no output displayed and user input unresponsive after live migration on Gen2 VM with Trusted Launch and Secure Boot.
rootCause: Serial Console and logs become unavailable after live migration for Gen2 VMs with Trusted Launch + Secure Boot enabled.
solution: Perform a guest OS restart to restore Serial Console functionality.

symptom: Azure Serial Console error: Another connection is currently in progress to this VM.
rootCause: Another user has Serial Console open, or a stale connection exists.
solution: If no other user has Serial Console open, try disabling and re-enabling boot diagnostics to restore access.

symptom: VM error: OSProvisioningTimedOut - OS provisioning did not finish in allotted time. VM guest agent detected but OS not properly prepared.
rootCause: Image not properly generalized (sysprep for Windows / waagent -deprovision for Linux), or provisioning took too long.
solution: Ensure image is properly prepared/generalized. If guest agent detected but times out, use VHD with CreateOption=Attach instead of FromImage.

symptom: VM error: VMAgentStatusCommunicationError - VM has not reported status for VM agent or extensions.
rootCause: VM agent is not running or VM cannot establish outbound connections to Azure storage.
solution: Verify VM has a running VM agent and can establish outbound connections to Azure storage. Check NSG rules and firewall.

symptom: VM error: StorageAccountTypeNotSupported - Boot diagnostics fails because premium storage account is used.
rootCause: Premium storage account type does not support boot diagnostics.
solution: Use a General purpose storage account for boot diagnostics instead of premium storage.

symptom: VM error: OSProvisioningClientError - SSH host key generation failed during Linux VM provisioning.
rootCause: Linux agent (waagent) not set up properly.
solution: Verify Linux agent is set up properly per https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/virtual-machines/extensions/agent-linux/

symptom: Computer name of Azure VM using specialized disk is blank in portal/PowerShell/CLI. OS profile properties (computerName, adminUsername, adminPassword) are read-only.
rootCause: When creating a VM from an existing or specialized disk, the OSProfile settings section is not included in the template (only used for generalized images). Azure portal reads computer name from OSProfile, so the field appears blank.
solution: This is a known Azure platform limitation. The VM name provided during resource creation is still displayed, and the hostname inside the guest OS is unchanged. No fix available — cosmetic issue only.

symptom: Pinging a Clustered Name (Failover Cluster Network Name or client access point IP) from non-owner nodes on Azure IaaS VMs returns 'Request timed out'.
rootCause: Expected behavior: Clustered IP address resources have limited functions in Azure networking and do not respond to ICMP ping. The Cluster Name IP is intended for management only, not general network communication. Client access point IPs are Azure Load Balancer addresses with specific port mappings.
solution: Use TCP connectivity tests instead of ICMP ping. For cluster management, connect locally using period (.) or node name. For file share creation, use the active cluster node. This is by-design behavior in Azure Failover Clustering.

symptom: Boot Diagnostics in Azure portal shows error: 'Failed to get contents of the log' when viewing VM screenshot. No SerialConsole.log in storage account.
rootCause: Multiple causes: (1) Windows Server 2008 R2 or earlier does not produce VM Health Report. (2) Old VM Guest Agent version (earlier than 2.7.1198.806) cannot generate report. (3) Unresolved SIDs in Local Administrators group cause VM Guest Agent health report generation to fail.
solution: (1) Upgrade OS to newer version. (2) Update VM Guest Agent to version 2.7.1198.822+. (3) Remove unresolved SIDs from Local Administrators group. (4) Redeploy VM to different host if serial port issue suspected.

symptom: Ubuntu 18.04 LTS VM no longer receives security/feature/maintenance updates after May 31, 2023 (end of standard support). VM becomes vulnerable.
rootCause: Canonical Ubuntu 18.04 LTS reached end of standard support on May 31, 2023. No more security patches from Canonical for the free tier.
solution: Three options: (1) Upgrade to Ubuntu 20.04 LTS then to 22.04 LTS (no direct 18.04→22.04 path). (2) Upgrade to Ubuntu Pro 18.04 for ESM support until April 2028 (in-place upgrade or Marketplace). (3) Deploy fresh Ubuntu 22.04 LTS VM. Note: in-place major version upgrade breaks control plane connection — Azure capabilities like Auto guest patching, Auto OS image upgrades, Hotpatching, Azure Update Manager become unavailable.

symptom: Cannot connect to Azure Linux VM after disabling the default NIC or manually setting a static IP for the NIC
rootCause: The default network interface (eth0) is disabled or has incorrect static IP configuration, preventing network connectivity
solution: Reset the NIC using Azure portal (change IP to static, assign new available IP, VM restarts), Azure PowerShell (Set-AzNetworkInterfaceIpConfig), or Azure CLI (az vm repair reset-nic). After connectivity restored, optionally revert to original IP.

symptom: CentOS VM: eth0 does not seem to be present / Device eth0 has different MAC address than expected / socket IOError No route to host / DoDhcpWork timeout after changing IP from DHCP to fixed
rootCause: HWADDR directive in /etc/sysconfig/network-scripts/ifcfg-eth0 contains old MAC address that doesn't match current NIC after VM resize/move, or IP was changed from DHCP to static incorrectly
solution: Remove the HWADDR directive from /etc/sysconfig/network-scripts/ifcfg-eth0

symptom: SUSE VM: No configuration found for eth4 / eth0 No interface found - VM not accessible over network
rootCause: Stale udev rules under /etc/udev/rules.d contain entries for old network interface names (eth0/eth1) that no longer match current hardware
solution: Remove the files that contain entries for eth0 or eth1 under /etc/udev/rules.d

symptom: Ubuntu VM: cloud-init-nonet waiting 120 seconds for a network device / route_info failed / Missing required variable: address for eth0/inet
rootCause: (Error 1) Outdated udev saves previous NIC as eth0, new NIC becomes eth1 due to MAC change after resize/move. (Error 2) /etc/network/interfaces.d/eth0.cfg has static config instead of DHCP
solution: (Error 1) Delete /etc/udev/rules.d/70-persistent-net.rules and update udev package. (Error 2) Reset eth0.cfg to 'auto eth0; iface eth0 inet dhcp' and remove cached DHCP files in /var/lib/dhcp

symptom: SSH connection to Linux VM fails with: /var/empty/sshd (RHEL) or /var/lib/empty (SUSE) or /var/run/sshd (Ubuntu) must be owned by root and not group or world-writable
rootCause: The sshd privilege separation directory does not exist, is not owned by root, or has incorrect permissions (group/world-writable)
solution: Create directory if missing, set permissions to 755, set ownership to root:root. For RHEL: mkdir -p /var/empty/sshd && chmod 755 && chown root:root. For SUSE: /var/lib/empty. For Ubuntu: /var/run/sshd (runs in memory, restart also fixes). Can use Serial Console, Run Command, or az vm repair.

symptom: SSH connection to Azure Linux VM fails, serial console shows 'Failed to load SELinux policy' and OS cannot complete boot
rootCause: SELinux misconfiguration in /etc/selinux/config, commonly SELINUXTYPE set to 'disabled' instead of 'targeted'/'minimum'/'mls'
solution: (1) Restart VM, interrupt at GRUB menu, add selinux=0 to kernel line to boot with SELinux off, then fix /etc/selinux/config. (2) Use rescue VM with az vm repair, mount OS disk via chroot, correct the config. SELINUXTYPE should be 'targeted', 'minimum', or 'mls'.

symptom: Cannot connect to specialized Debian 9.1 VM after migrating from VMware to Azure
rootCause: NIC name is 'ens33p0' instead of 'eth0' which is required by Azure Linux network configuration. Debian uses predictable network interface names.
solution: Modify GRUB: add 'net.ifnames=0 biosdevname=0' to GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX. Update /etc/network/interfaces: remove ens33 entry, add 'auto eth0' and 'iface inet eth0 dhcp'.

symptom: Cannot SSH to Azure Linux VM, syslog shows: Permissions 0777 for '/etc/ssh/sshKeyName' are too open / This private key will be ignored / key_load_private: bad permissions
rootCause: The /etc/ssh configuration directory or files have incorrect permissions (e.g., 0777), accessible by users other than owner. Usually caused by incorrect chmod command.
solution: Restore correct permissions: chmod -R 644 /etc/ssh; chmod 600 /etc/ssh/ssh_host*key; chmod 600 /etc/ssh/sshd_config; chmod 755 /home/<user>; chmod 700 /home/<user>/.ssh; chmod 600 authorized_keys. Use Run Command, Serial Console, or offline repair with az vm repair.

symptom: Linux VM encounters page allocation failure (GFP_ATOMIC) indicating memory allocation failure under atomic context
rootCause: System runs out of available memory pages; can be caused by unexpected load spikes, memory fragmentation, memory leaks, misconfigured memory parameters, kernel bugs, or lack of swap space
solution: Diagnose with free/vmstat/top/htop/cat /proc/meminfo/buddyinfo. Scale up VM memory, reconfigure cgroup limits, adjust huge page allocation, or configure swap space on ephemeral disk via cloud-init or waagent.conf

symptom: Linux VM logs Out of memory: Kill process or invoked oom-killer - OOM Killer terminates processes
rootCause: Both RAM and swap are exhausted; OOM Killer selects process with highest oom_score/RSS. Causes: memory leaks, too many processes, insufficient VM size
solution: Identify high-memory process via top/ps. Check for memory leaks. Scale up VM size. Configure swap on ephemeral disk. Reconfigure cgroup memory limits

symptom: Linux VM logs Failed to fork: Cannot allocate memory - systemd or applications cannot create new processes
rootCause: Insufficient free memory to allocate initial process state for new fork. System under severe memory pressure
solution: Scale up VM memory, configure swap space, identify and fix memory-consuming processes or leaks, adjust cgroup/overcommit settings

symptom: Linux VM fails to boot with Kernel panic - VFS: Unable to mount root fs on unknown-block(0,0) or missing initramfs image
rootCause: Missing initramfs image after kernel update/patching. Initramfs file not generated or GRUB config has missing initrd entry
solution: Boot previous kernel via Serial Console GRUB menu. Regenerate initramfs: depmod + dracut. Regenerate GRUB config. Or use ALAR scripts: az vm repair repair-button --button-command initrd

symptom: Linux VM fails to boot with Kernel panic - Attempted to kill init! after dracut warning
rootCause: Missing important files/directories, missing system core libraries (libc.so.6), wrong file permissions (chmod 777 on /), missing partitions, or SELinux issues
solution: Attach OS disk to repair VM + chroot. For missing libs: rpm --verify + reinstall packages. For wrong permissions: rpm -a --setperms. For SELinux: add selinux=0 then touch /.autorelabel. For missing partitions: recreate with fdisk/gdisk

symptom: Linux VM kernel panic after kernel upgrade/downgrade - kernel BUG or boot failure with new kernel version
rootCause: Kernel upgrade incompatibility or bug; kernel downgrade left missing modules; kernel module changes caused panic
solution: Boot previous kernel via Serial Console or ALAR scripts (az vm repair run --run-id linux-alar2 --parameters kernel). Change default kernel with grubby. Install latest kernel from vendor. Check modules with lsinitrd/lsmod

symptom: Ubuntu VM apt update/install fails with connection timeout to azure.archive.ubuntu.com when VM is behind an internal standard load balancer
rootCause: Internal standard load balancer does not provide default outbound connectivity.
solution: Add public IP, use external LB, configure NAT gateway, or add SNAT outbound rules.

symptom: apt update fails with 470 status code or The repository is no longer signed error on Ubuntu VM
rootCause: Firewall, virtual appliance, or NSG blocking outbound traffic to Ubuntu repo URLs on ports 80/443.
solution: Allow azure.archive.ubuntu.com and packages.microsoft.com through firewall. Add ports 80/443 to NSG outbound allow rules.

symptom: apt update fails with GPG signature error: NO_PUBKEY for third-party repository
rootCause: Third-party repository added but its GPG public key was not imported.
solution: Import GPG key using curl to /etc/apt/trusted.gpg.d/. Use gpg --dearmor to convert. If key not found, remove the repo entry.

symptom: apt update fails with Temporary failure resolving azure.archive.ubuntu.com
rootCause: Custom DNS resolver not functioning correctly, or DNS server in different subnet without proper UDR.
solution: Switch to Azure default DNS to test. Verify custom DNS on port 53. If DNS in different subnet, ensure correct UDR.

symptom: dpkg error processing linux-image during kernel install: grub-mkconfig Syntax error EOF in backquote substitution
rootCause: Syntax error in /etc/default/grub (e.g., missing closing quotation mark in GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX).
solution: Fix syntax error in /etc/default/grub, then retry kernel package installation.

symptom: apt update fails with does not have a Release file for armhf packages on x86_64 Azure VM
rootCause: /etc/apt/sources.list contains ARM (armhf) architecture repos, invalid for x86_64 VMs.
solution: Remove or comment out armhf repository lines from sources.list or sources.list.d/*.list.

symptom: apt update fails with Unknown error executing apt-key and GPG signature verification errors
rootCause: Permission issues on /etc/apt/trusted.gpg.d/*.gpg - not readable by _apt user. Often caused by umask 0777.
solution: chmod 644 /etc/apt/trusted.gpg.d/*.gpg. Verify umask in /etc/login.defs is 0022.

symptom: Gen2 Linux VM fails to boot: boot loader did not load an operating system / boot image not found - UEFI partition missing
rootCause: UEFI (EFI System) boot loader partition missing or deleted from OS disk.
solution: Create repair VM via az vm repair, recreate EFI partition with gdisk (correct sector points per distro), restore via az vm repair restore.

symptom: Gen2 Linux VM fails to boot - UEFI EFI partition corrupted (dirty bit set)
rootCause: UEFI vfat partition corrupted, preventing boot loader from reading EFI System partition.
solution: Create repair VM, run fsck.vfat on EFI partition to remove dirty bit, then restore OS disk.

symptom: Linux VM fails to boot after entire /boot partition contents deleted
rootCause: All /boot contents (kernel, initramfs, grub) deleted and unrecoverable.
solution: Restore VM from backup using Azure Backup - only recovery option.

symptom: Cloud-init Linux VM takes over 2 minutes to start: ephemeral device did not appear after 120 seconds
rootCause: VM size has no local ephemeral disks (Ev4, Dv5, Dasv5). Cloud-init waits 120s for non-existent disk.
solution: Upgrade cloud-init to 21.2+. Workaround: sed maxwait from 120 to 2 in DataSourceAzure.py.

symptom: Cloud-init intermittently writes SSH Host keys to console as ec2 instead of Azure, causing confusion during troubleshooting
rootCause: Cloud-init SSH module calls write-ssh-key-fingerprints helper which uses legacy program name ec2 from original ec2-init project name
solution: Ignore ec2 logs during troubleshooting; this is cosmetic only and does not affect VM functionality

symptom: Cannot resize MBR partition for data disk larger than 2 TB using fdisk; partition stays at 2 TB maximum
rootCause: MBR partition table has 32-bit sector address limitation (2^32 sectors), limiting partition size to 2 TB for 512-byte sector disks
solution: Convert partition from MBR to GPT using gdisk tool: backup data, unmount filesystem, use gdisk to delete and recreate partition as GPT, run fsck, remount and resize filesystem with resize2fs or xfs_growfs

symptom: Azure Linux CustomScript extension 2.0.5 reruns the previous command unexpectedly after auto-update
rootCause: Bug in Microsoft.Azure.Extensions.CustomScript 2.0.5 extension causes previous command to rerun during auto-update from 2.0.x
solution: Uninstall CustomScript extension to avoid rerun, or wait for auto-update to 2.0.6 which fixes the issue

symptom: Linux VM extensions stop running; extension downgrade error in /var/log/waagent.log: Downgrade not allowed; affects Azure Backup and Site Recovery
rootCause: Bug in Linux VM Agent versions 2.2.21 to 2.2.24 prevents extension processing due to downgrade check failure
solution: Remove all *.manifest.xml files in /var/lib/waagent/ to resolve immediately; alternatively wait for Microsoft auto-fix via RunCommandLinux extension

symptom: GPU disabled on Azure N-Series Ubuntu 16.04 LTS VM after upgrading to 4.4.0-75 kernel; kernel NULL pointer dereference BUG in nvidia module
rootCause: Incompatibility between NVIDIA driver and Ubuntu 4.4.0-75 kernel causing kernel NULL pointer dereference in nvidia module
solution: Upgrade kernel to at least version 4.4.0-77

symptom: Linux VM fails to boot after migration to Azure or after disabling Hyper-V drivers. VM stuck at dracut/initramfs shell, kernel panic, disk not found errors, no network connectivity. Serial console shows: Warning: /dev/mapper/rootvg-rootlv does not exist or UUID does not exist, dropping to shell.
rootCause: Hyper-V LIS drivers (hv_vmbus, hv_storvsc, hv_netvsc, hv_utils) missing, disabled, or not included in initramfs. Common after migrating from on-premises VMware/KVM to Azure, or after manually disabling hv_ drivers in /etc/modprobe.d/
solution: Solution 1: Create repair VM, mount OS disk via chroot, find and comment out hv_ disable entries: grep -nr hv_ /etc/modprobe.d/ then edit the file. Solution 2: For missing drivers in initramfs, add hv_vmbus hv_netvsc hv_storvsc to /etc/dracut.conf (RHEL/SLES: add_drivers+="hv_vmbus hv_netvsc hv_storvsc") or /etc/initramfs-tools/modules (Ubuntu), then rebuild initramfs via dracut/update-initramfs.

symptom: SLES 12 to 15 migration fails with error: The requested products HPC Module 12 x86_64 are not activated on the system, or Invalid combination of products registered.
rootCause: HPC became a standalone product in SLES 15. The HPC module is no longer available to systems registered as SLES, preventing zypper migration from finding a valid target.
solution: Remove the HPC module before migration: sudo zypper rm sle-module-hpc-release-POOL sle-module-hpc-release. Alternatively, move sle-module-hpc.prod from /etc/products.d/ to /tmp/ and retry zypper migration.

symptom: SLES 12 to 15 migration fails: suse-migration-sle15-activation not found in package names. No provider of suse-migration-sle15-activation found.
rootCause: SLES 12 Public Cloud module is not enabled by default.
solution: Enable Public Cloud module: sudo SUSEConnect -p sle-module-public-cloud/12/x86_64. On SAP instances, remove sle-ha-release and sle-ha-release-POOL first. Then cleanup: SUSEConnect --cleanup, rm /etc/zypp/{credentials,services,repos}.d/*, rm /var/cache/zypp/*, rm /var/lib/cloudregister/*, registercloudguest --force-new.

symptom: Generation 2 Azure VM does not start after SLES 15 SP1 to SP2 upgrade. Serial console shows: error: symbol grub_file_filters not found or error: symbol grub_verify string not found when loading kernel.
rootCause: After Generation 2 VM restart/stop/dealloc, Hyper-V in Azure does not preserve startup entries. The SUSE Linux VM cannot start due to corrupted GRUB entries.
solution: Create rescue VM from OS disk snapshot, set up chroot, reinstall GRUB bootloader: sudo /usr/sbin/shim-install --config-file=/boot/grub2/grub.cfg. Then swap snapshot disk back.

symptom: SLES 15 to SP3 migration fails: The requested products are not activated on the system. Migration was previously interrupted or terminated.
rootCause: Incomplete package updates from an interrupted SLES migration causing version mismatch.
solution: Roll back packages to compatible versions: sudo zypper dup, sudo zypper rollback, then retry sudo zypper migration.

symptom: After SLES migration, VM does not start with latest kernel. Repositories are not defined and registration fails.
rootCause: /etc/credentials.d directory has incorrect permissions or corrupted file content.
solution: Clean up registration: rm /var/cache/cloudregister/, rm /etc/zypp/credentials.d/, chmod 0755 /etc/zypp/credentials.d*, registercloudguest --force-new. Then zypper update and reboot.

symptom: SLES 12 SP5 to 15 SP1 DMS migration fails with: Not ready to read within timeout, skipping multiple repositories (HPC, Legacy, Public Cloud, Containers).
rootCause: regionService directory moved from /var/lib to /usr/lib, but DMS scripting looks for certs only under /var/lib.
solution: Create old directory: mkdir -p /var/lib/regionService/certs. Copy certs: cp -a /usr/lib/regionService/certs/* /var/lib/regionService/certs/. Edit /etc/regionserverclnt.cfg to set certLocation = /var/lib/regionService/certs. Then install latest SLES15-Migration and retry zypper migration.

symptom: SLES 12 SP5 to 15 SP1 migration fails with: IsADirectoryError: [Errno 21] Is a directory: /system-root/etc/pki/trust/anchors/temp. suse-migration-prepare.service fails.
rootCause: Unknown folder (temp) exists in /etc/pki/trust/anchors directory causing migration preparation to fail.
solution: Move temp folder: sudo mv /etc/pki/trust/anchors/temp /backuplocation/temp. Install migration package: sudo zypper install suse-migration-sle15-activation. Retry zypper migration.

symptom: SLES 15 SP3 to SP4 migration succeeds but SP4 to SP5 fails: No migration available. Products listed as (not available). Shows SP3 modules still registered instead of SP4.
rootCause: Required modules not properly activated/deactivated during intermediate migration, causing module version mismatch.
solution: Before migration: activate modules (web-scripting, public-cloud, containers, live-patching) and deactivate legacy/python2/PackageHub modules. Then SUSEConnect --cleanup, rm credentials/services/repos/cache/cloudregister, registercloudguest --force-new.

symptom: SLES 15 SP3 to SP4 migration fails: Invalid system credentials, probably because the registered system was deleted in SUSE Customer Center. No repositories defined.
rootCause: Certification module existence causes credential validation failure.
solution: Disable certification module before migration: sudo SUSEConnect -d -p sle-module-certifications/15.3/x86_64. Then retry zypper migration.

symptom: SLES 15 SP3 to SP6 zypper migration fails: Signature verification failed for repomd.xml. Skipping repository SLE-Product-SLES15-SP6-Updates. Migration failed with rollback.
rootCause: In mid-2023 SUSE changed GPG signing key for SLE 15 products to RSA 4096 bit key. Old systems lack the new key.
solution: Import new SUSE GPG key: create repo-signing-key-sle-15.txt with the RSA 4096 public key block, then rpm --import repo-signing-key-sle-15.txt. Verify with rpm -q gpg-pubkey (should show 3fa1d6ce-63c9481c). Then retry zypper migration.

symptom: Linux VM fails to boot after migration to Azure or after disabling Hyper-V drivers. VM stuck at dracut/initramfs, kernel panic, cannot find root device, no network, GRUB failure. Missing hv_vmbus/hv_storvsc/hv_netvsc.
rootCause: Hyper-V/LIS drivers not installed, disabled in /etc/modprobe.d, or missing from initramfs. Common after VMware/KVM migration.
solution: Fix1(disabled): chroot repair VM, grep -nr hv_ /etc/modprobe.d/, remove blacklist. Fix2(missing): add hv_vmbus hv_netvsc hv_storvsc to /etc/dracut.conf or /etc/initramfs-tools/modules, rebuild initramfs.

symptom: SLES 12->15 migration fails: HPC Module 12 x86_64 not activated or Invalid combination of products registered
rootCause: HPC became standalone product in SLES 15, blocks migration target resolution.
solution: Remove HPC: zypper rm sle-module-hpc-release-POOL sle-module-hpc-release. Or mv /etc/products.d/sle-module-hpc.prod /tmp/

symptom: SLES migration: suse-migration-sle15-activation not found in package names
rootCause: SLES 12 Public Cloud module not enabled by default.
solution: SUSEConnect -p sle-module-public-cloud/12/x86_64. For SAP remove sle-ha-release. Cleanup: SUSEConnect --cleanup, registercloudguest --force-new.

symptom: SLES Gen2 VM won t start after SP1->SP2 upgrade: grub_file_filters not found or grub_verify string not found
rootCause: After Gen2 VM stop/deallocate, Hyper-V doesn t preserve startup entries, GRUB corrupted.
solution: Chroot rescue VM, reinstall GRUB: /usr/sbin/shim-install --config-file=/boot/grub2/grub.cfg

symptom: SLES 15->SP3 migration fails: products not activated, list of SP1 modules not activated
rootCause: Previous SLES migration interrupted/terminated, incomplete package updates.
solution: zypper dup, zypper rollback, zypper migration to retry.

symptom: After SLES migration, VM won t boot with latest kernel, repositories not defined
rootCause: /etc/credentials.d has incorrect permissions or corrupted content.
solution: rm /var/cache/cloudregister/ /etc/zypp/credentials.d/, chmod 0755 /etc/zypp/credentials.d*, registercloudguest --force-new, zypper update, reboot.

symptom: SLES 12 SP5->15 SP1 migration: Not ready to read within timeout for multiple repos
rootCause: regionService dir moved from /var/lib to /usr/lib, DMS only looks under /var/lib for certs.
solution: mkdir -p /var/lib/regionService/certs, cp certs from /usr/lib, update certLocation in /etc/regionserverclnt.cfg.

symptom: SLES migration: IsADirectoryError Errno 21 /system-root/etc/pki/trust/anchors/temp in suse-migration-prepare
rootCause: Unexpected directory in /etc/pki/trust/anchors breaks migration script.
solution: mv /etc/pki/trust/anchors/temp to backup, install suse-migration-sle15-activation, retry.

symptom: SLES 15 SP3->SP4: Invalid system credentials and No repositories defined
rootCause: Certification module conflicts with migration, causes invalid credentials.
solution: SUSEConnect -d -p sle-module-certifications/15.3/x86_64 then retry.

symptom: SLES SP3->SP4/SP5: No migration available, products not available after incomplete migration
rootCause: Required modules not activated while obsolete modules block path.
solution: Activate web-scripting/public-cloud/containers/live-patching, deactivate legacy/python2/PackageHub, SUSEConnect --cleanup, registercloudguest --force-new.

symptom: SLES 12 SP5->15 SP6: Signature verification failed for repomd.xml after intermediate SP3 migration
rootCause: SUSE changed GPG signing key to RSA 4096 mid-2023, old systems lack new key.
solution: Import new GPG key (3fa1d6ce-63c9481c): rpm --import repo-signing-key-sle-15.txt, verify with rpm -q gpg-pubkey, retry zypper migration.

symptom: Linux VM fails to boot due to ext4 filesystem corruption. Serial log shows EXT4-fs error, IO failure, or bad extra_isize. VM enters emergency mode.
rootCause: Disk corruption on ext4 filesystem caused by kernel problems, driver errors, or hardware issues.
solution: Identify corrupted disk via serial log. Use fsck /dev/sdX to repair ext4. Repeat fsck until clean. Offline: attach OS disk to rescue VM. Add nofail to non-critical mounts.

symptom: Linux VM fails to boot due to XFS filesystem corruption. Serial log shows XFS Metadata CRC error.
rootCause: XFS disk corruption. Uncommitted journal entries or metadata CRC errors.
solution: xfs_repair -n to check, xfs_repair to fix. Journal error: try mount first. If fails, xfs_repair -L (data loss risk). Offline: rescue VM.

symptom: Linux VM boots into emergency mode due to fstab misconfiguration. Timed out waiting for device, Dependency failed.
rootCause: Incorrect /etc/fstab: device names instead of UUID, wrong UUID, missing nofail, syntax errors.
solution: Serial Console single-user mode, fix fstab (UUID+nofail), mount -a, reboot. ALAR: az vm repair repair-button --button-command fstab.

symptom: Linux VM SSH fails, networking down. hv_netvsc disabled in /etc/modprobe.d/. Interface not found for DHCP.
rootCause: Hyper-V network driver (hv_netvsc) disabled via blacklist in /etc/modprobe.d/.
solution: grep -nr hv_netvsc /etc/modprobe.d/, remove disable entries, dracut -f -v or mkinitramfs, reboot.

symptom: Linux VM networking fails after NIC change. MAC address mismatch, cloud-init apply_network_config false.
rootCause: NIC MAC changed but OS config not updated. cloud-init apply_network_config disabled.
solution: Set apply_network_config: true in cloud-init cfg. Or rm /var/lib/cloud/instance/obj.pkl and reboot.

symptom: Linux VM drops to dracut shell. dracut-initqueue timeout, rootvg-rootlv does not exist.
rootCause: hv_vmbus/hv_storvsc disabled in /etc/modprobe.d/. initramfs cannot access root fs.
solution: Offline: rescue VM, chroot, remove hv driver disable entries, dracut -f -v, swap disk.

symptom: Linux VM 3.10 kernel (RHEL/CentOS 7.0-7.1) panics after host memory-preserving update. SCSI sysfs trace.
rootCause: SCSI locking bug in kernel < 3.10.0-327.10.1 triggered by Hyper-V host disk hot-remove.
solution: Restart VM. Update kernel to 3.10.0-327.10.1+ (RHEL/CentOS/Oracle 7.2).

symptom: Linux VM boot fails or apps break after restart because /dev/sdX device names changed.
rootCause: Linux device paths not persistent across reboots. SCSI async scan reorders devices.
solution: Use persistent naming: UUID in fstab, /dev/disk/azure/scsiN/lunN, or LVM names. Add nofail. Update udev rules.

symptom: Oracle Linux 8 SP2 VM: Failed to start Switch Root after GRUB package update.
rootCause: GRUB update corrupts boot entries. kernelopts missing from /boot/loader/entries/.
solution: Boot rescue kernel, copy kernelopts from grubenv, add to boot entry + earlyprintk=ttyS0, reboot.

symptom: Linux VM does not start correctly after LIS 4.1.3 upgrade on kernel 3.10.0-514.16, depmod warnings about unknown symbols
rootCause: Kernel ABI changes in kernel 3.10.0-514.16.1 cause LIS 4.1.3 driver upgrade to fail
solution: Do not upgrade LIS 4.1.3 on kernel 3.10.0-514.16.1. Install LIS 4.2.0 or later.

symptom: Linux VM shuts down or fails to boot when OS disk /var/log/audit partition is full. auditd HALT config triggers shutdown
rootCause: auditd.conf admin_space_left_action=HALT causes system shutdown when audit log disk is full
solution: Change HALT to SUSPEND in /etc/audit/auditd.conf via rescue VM. Free disk space (10% free). Use ALAR auditd action.

symptom: Linux VM fails to boot after OS disk resize. cloud-init Errno 28 No space left on device
rootCause: Cloud-init requires some free space to expand root filesystem. Full disk prevents resize
solution: Clear unneeded data via rescue VM or single user mode, then expand filesystem

symptom: Linux VM fails to boot after VFAT disabled. Failed to mount /boot/efi. ADE VMs cannot mount BEK VOLUME
rootCause: VFAT required for /boot/efi (Gen2) and ADE BEK VOLUME. Hardening tools disable via /etc/modprobe.d/
solution: Remove 'install vfat /bin/true' from /etc/modprobe.d/, regenerate initramfs. Gen1 non-encrypted can comment /boot/efi in fstab

symptom: Linux VM fails to boot due to incorrect HugePages. OOM, udev failure, or Kernel panic deadlocked on memory
rootCause: vm.nr_hugepages in /etc/sysctl.conf set too high, reserving all memory for HugePages
solution: Boot single user mode or rescue VM. Reduce vm.nr_hugepages in /etc/sysctl.conf. Regenerate initramfs. Upgrade VM size if needed.

symptom: Swap file not re-created after Linux VM restart on cloud-init provisioned VMs with waagent
rootCause: Both cloud-init and waagent try to configure swap, causing conflict
solution: Disable swap in waagent (ResourceDisk.EnableSwap=n). Create cloud-init per-boot script for swap.

symptom: Cannot access Gen2 Linux VM after reboot. Password authentication fails.
rootCause: Bug in cloud-init 19.3 and earlier on Gen2 VMs breaks password auth after reboot
solution: Upgrade cloud-init to 19.4+. If locked out, use VM Access extension or portal Password Reset.

symptom: Linux VM deployment fails with OSProvisioningInternalError. VM stuck in Creating state ~40min then fails
rootCause: Missing/misconfigured provisioning agent, incorrect image config, or disabled UDF module
solution: Enable boot diagnostics, check serial log. Ensure UDF not blocked. Verify cloud-init/waagent configured. Check /var/tmp not noexec.

symptom: Allocation failure when deploying custom/gallery/marketplace Linux image. Cluster cannot support requested VM size or has no free resources
rootCause: New VM request pinned to cluster that cannot support the VM size or has no available free space
solution: Retry with smaller VM size, or stop all VMs in availability set first then create new VM in desired size. Or create VM in different availability set in same region.

symptom: Provisioning timeout or failure when deploying custom Linux image. Generalized image uploaded as specialized or vice versa
rootCause: Image type mismatch: generalized image uploaded/captured as specialized causes provisioning timeout; specialized as generalized causes provisioning failure with original credentials retained
solution: Upload/capture with correct setting matching OS state. Run -deprovision before uploading as generalized. Recapture from current VHDs with correct generalized/specialized setting.

symptom: Azure Linux VM maximum memory limited to 64 GB despite larger VM size. free -m shows only 64GB inside VM
rootCause: Bug in Linux kernel prior to 3.10 with MTRR handling on Windows Server 2016 Hyper-V hosts (16TB physical address space). Affects SLES 11 SP4, RHEL 6.x, CentOS 6.x
solution: Add disable_mtrr_trim as kernel boot line option in GRUB config. Or upgrade to newer kernel (SLES 12, RHEL 7.x, CentOS 7.x).

symptom: RHEL Pacemaker cluster node does not join after restart. Quorum error, nodes UNCLEAN (offline). Corosync quorum is not configured error in /var/log/messages
rootCause: Missing or incorrect quorum section in /etc/corosync/corosync.conf (VoteQuorum provider not configured)
solution: Add quorum { provider: corosync_votequorum; two_node: 1 } to corosync.conf. Put cluster in maintenance, sync and reload corosync.

symptom: Pacemaker VIP (IPaddr2) resource fails with [findif] failed error
rootCause: NIC is down or no matching route for VIP IP/netmask in default routing table, so findif() cannot determine which NIC to use
solution: Specify nic=eth0 in IPaddr2 resource config: pcs resource update vip_XXX nic=eth0

symptom: Fenced Pacemaker node does not rejoin cluster. Logs show: We were allegedly just fenced
rootCause: Node rejoins Corosync membership too quickly after fencing. STONITH completion message arrives after node has already restarted cluster services, causing it to shut down again
solution: Add startup delay for Corosync: create systemd drop-in with ExecStartPre=/bin/sleep 60. Or use priority-fencing-delay / pcmk_delay_max parameter.

symptom: Both Pacemaker cluster nodes terminated after failover in RHEL 8 two-node cluster
rootCause: During outage, both nodes try to fence each other simultaneously via STONITH device
solution: Use priority-fencing-delay=15s (Pacemaker 2.0.4-6+) or pcmk_delay_max=15s (older) to ensure only one node wins the fencing race

symptom: Azure fence agent fails in SUSE Pacemaker. Missing fence-agents-azure-arm package after Python 3.11 update
rootCause: SUSE rebuilt fence agent package for Python 3.11. Old package not compatible.
solution: Install fence-agents-azure-arm package: zypper in fence-agents-azure-arm on all nodes

symptom: SUSE Pacemaker does not start after SysRq-triggered reboot. SBD msgwait conflict with fast Azure VM restart
rootCause: SBD_DELAY_START=no causes conflict when VMs reboot faster than SBD msgwait timeout
solution: Set SBD_DELAY_START=yes in /etc/sysconfig/sbd. May need to adjust sbd.service TimeoutSec.

symptom: Azure Linux VM Agent 2.1.5 or 2.1.6 cannot process extensions after multiple auto-updates. High CPU by waagent.
rootCause: Bug in Agent 2.1.5/2.1.6 where multiple auto-updates corrupt state.
solution: Upgrade Agent via distro package manager. Workaround: restart walinuxagent service.

symptom: Linux VM Guest Agent Not Ready. Extensions fail. walinuxagent service stopped.
rootCause: Service not running, auto-update disabled, or VM cannot reach WireServer 168.63.129.16 (firewall/iptables blocking ports 80,443,32526).
solution: Check service status, restart, verify AutoUpdate.Enabled=y, test curl 168.63.129.16, check iptables.

symptom: waagent.log: error retrieving goal state. Cannot connect to WireServer 168.63.129.16.
rootCause: Firewall/proxy/iptables blocking WireServer IP on ports 80, 32526.
solution: curl http://168.63.129.16/?comp=versions to test. Check iptables, proxy, third-party firewall.

symptom: RHEL yum update timeout to rhui-3/rhui4-1.microsoft.com. Forced tunneling/VPN reroutes RHUI traffic.
rootCause: ExpressRoute/VPN forced tunneling routes RHUI to on-premises. RHUI only accepts Azure public IP traffic.
solution: Create UDR for RHUI 4 IPs with next hop Internet. Per-region IPs: West Europe 52.136.197.163, East US 52.142.4.99, SE Asia 20.24.186.80.

symptom: RHEL yum timeout. VM behind internal standard load balancer, no outbound connectivity to RHUI.
rootCause: Internal standard LB does not provide outbound connectivity.
solution: Add public IP, use external LB, add NAT gateway, or configure SNAT rules.

symptom: RHEL yum fails. Firewall/NVA/NSG blocking RHUI. SSL inspection altering RHUI cert.
rootCause: Firewall/NVA or NSG blocking RHUI IPs. SSL inspection altering certificate.
solution: Allow RHUI 4 IPs in firewall. Add to NSG outbound allow. Whitelist RHUI URLs in SSL inspection.

symptom: RHEL yum fails through proxy. Misconfigured or residual proxy in /etc/yum.conf.
rootCause: Wrong proxy config in /etc/yum.conf. Or leftover proxy= when no proxy exists.
solution: Set correct proxy= or remove proxy= lines from yum.conf/dnf.conf.

symptom: Extension status not reported after Linux Agent 2.2.19 update.
rootCause: Known issue in Agent 2.2.19: goalstate1.xml missing.
solution: Microsoft auto-deploys RunCommandLinux to fix. Verify: az vm extension list.

symptom: ADE Linux boot failure. Emergency mode. /dev/mapper/rootvg-rootlv missing. ADE modules not in initramfs.
rootCause: 91adeOnline (RHEL) or crypt-ade-boot/hook (Ubuntu) missing from initramfs.
solution: Repair VM + chroot. RHEL: copy 91adeOnline, dracut -f. Ubuntu: copy crypt-ade-boot/hook, update-initramfs -u -k all.

symptom: ADE encryption interrupted. Linux VM stuck in emergency mode after failed ADE deployment.
rootCause: ADE deployment failed/interrupted midway. Python errors or device not found in extension logs.
solution: Restore from backup. Or: az vm repair, check prerequisites, LUKS header, initramfs, BEK key file.

symptom: Ubuntu ADE fails. Boot partition too small for initramfs regeneration.
rootCause: ADE splits /boot; insufficient space for new initramfs. Ubuntu-specific.
solution: Delete old kernels. Ubuntu 24+ has 1GB+ boot partition.

symptom: ADE fails. VFAT module disabled, BEK volume unmountable, key inaccessible.
rootCause: VFAT kernel module disabled. BEK volume (FAT) cannot mount.
solution: Enable VFAT kernel module.

symptom: ADE encrypted Linux OS disk unrecoverable. osluksheader missing/corrupted.
rootCause: LUKS header file osluksheader corrupted/missing. Without it, partition cannot be unlocked.
solution: Restore osluksheader from backup. No backup = unrecoverable.

symptom: yum/dnf dependency conflict. Package requires version X but providers cannot be installed.
rootCause: Incompatible packages from different OS releases coexisting.
solution: yum remove <old-pkg>. Or --allowerasing/--skip-broken/--nobest.

symptom: yum SyntaxError/No module named yum/bad interpreter. Wrong Python version.
rootCause: Default Python changed or symlink modified.
solution: Fix symlink: ln -s python2.7 python. Or rpm -ivh python --replacepkgs.

symptom: yum/dnf duplicate packages after interrupted update. Protected multilib errors.
rootCause: Update interrupted (SSH drop, Ctrl+C, restart) leaving old+new versions.
solution: RHEL7: yum-complete-transaction --cleanup-only. RHEL8/9: yum remove --duplicates.

symptom: yum/dnf 404 Not Found for repomd.xml. Wrong releasever or EUS/non-EUS mismatch.
rootCause: Incorrect releasever in /etc/yum/vars/ or /etc/dnf/vars/. EUS/non-EUS repo mismatch.
solution: EUS: set correct version lock. Non-EUS: clear releasever. OL: clear ociregion. CentOS: rm releasever.

symptom: rpm/yum BDB0113 Thread died in Berkeley DB. Cannot open Packages database.
rootCause: Corrupted RPM database. Concurrent access, disk space, or abrupt termination.
solution: rm -f /var/lib/rpm/__db.*; rpmdb_verify Packages; rpm -vv --rebuilddb.

symptom: yum RHEL 7 HTTPS 403 Forbidden to RHUI. Third-party curl from city-fan.org.
rootCause: Third-party curl certificates not recognized by Red Hat.
solution: Downgrade to official RHEL curl: yum downgrade curl libcurl --disablerepo=*.

symptom: apt update/install fails with connection timeout to azure.archive.ubuntu.com. Errors: Could not connect to azure.archive.ubuntu.com:80, connection timed out.
rootCause: VM lacks outbound internet connectivity. Causes: internal Standard LB without outbound rules, external LB missing SNAT, firewall/NVA blocking Ubuntu repo URLs, private subnet without NAT gateway, or misconfigured proxy.
solution: Ensure outbound connectivity: add public IP, use NAT gateway, configure LB outbound rules, allow azure.archive.ubuntu.com in firewall/NSG (ports 80/443), or fix proxy in /etc/apt/apt.conf.

symptom: apt update fails with 470 status code when accessing azure.archive.ubuntu.com. Also: The repository is no longer signed.
rootCause: Firewall or NSG blocking HTTP/HTTPS traffic to Ubuntu repository URLs.
solution: Allow Ubuntu repo URLs through firewall. If NSG, add allow rules for ports 80/443 with higher priority than Block_Internet_Access_outbound.

symptom: apt update: signatures could not be verified, public key not available NO_PUBKEY for third-party repos.
rootCause: Third-party repo added to sources.list without importing its GPG public key.
solution: Import GPG key: curl -fsSL <key-url> | gpg --dearmor | sudo tee /etc/apt/trusted.gpg.d/<repo>.gpg > /dev/null. Or remove repo entry.

symptom: apt update: Temporary failure resolving azure.archive.ubuntu.com.
rootCause: Custom DNS resolver not working or DNS server on different subnet without proper UDR routing.
solution: Verify custom DNS. Try Azure default DNS at NIC level. Ensure UDR for cross-subnet DNS. Check port 53.

symptom: Kernel install via apt fails: dpkg error processing linux-image, /etc/default/grub Syntax error EOF in backquote substitution.
rootCause: Syntax error in /etc/default/grub (missing closing quote). Kernel post-install grub-mkconfig fails.
solution: Fix syntax in /etc/default/grub, ensure proper closing quotes. Re-run kernel installation.

symptom: apt update: repository does not have a Release file, pointing to binary-armhf/Packages on x86_64 VM.
rootCause: ARM (armhf) architecture repo entries in sources.list on x86_64 VM.
solution: Remove or comment out armhf lines from /etc/apt/sources.list or sources.list.d/.

symptom: apt update: Unknown error executing apt-key. Debug: key(s) in keyring ignored, not readable by user _apt.
rootCause: GPG key files under /etc/apt/trusted.gpg.d/ have wrong permissions. Caused by umask 0777.
solution: Fix: sudo chmod 644 /etc/apt/trusted.gpg.d/*.gpg. Check umask in /etc/login.defs is 0022.

symptom: Gen2 Linux VM UEFI boot failure: boot diagnostics shows The boot loader did not load an operating system or PXE boot.
rootCause: EFI System Partition (ESP) missing or deleted from OS disk.
solution: Rescue VM (az vm repair create), recreate EFI partition with gdisk per distro sector points (RHEL7: 2048-1026047, Ubuntu: 10240-227327, SLES: 6144-1054719). Type EF00. Swap disk back.

symptom: Gen2 Linux VM UEFI boot failure. fsck.vfat on EFI partition: Dirty bit set, not properly unmounted.
rootCause: UEFI boot partition (vfat) corrupted from improper shutdown.
solution: Rescue VM, attach OS disk copy, run fsck.vfat -n (dry run) then fsck.vfat to fix. Swap disk back.

symptom: Cannot connect to Debian 9.1 VM after VMware to Azure migration. SSH/network unreachable.
rootCause: Debian uses predictable NIC names (ens33) instead of eth0. Azure requires eth0.
solution: Update GRUB: add net.ifnames=0 biosdevname=0. Update /etc/network/interfaces: replace ens33 with auto eth0, iface eth0 inet dhcp.

symptom: Linux VM network lost, eth0 not configured. CentOS: Device eth0 does not seem to be present. SUSE: eth0 No interface found. Ubuntu: cloud-init-nonet waiting for network device.
rootCause: Distro-specific NIC config issues after resize/move: CentOS stale HWADDR; SUSE stale udev rules; Ubuntu outdated 70-persistent-net.rules or static IP.
solution: CentOS: remove HWADDR from ifcfg-eth0. SUSE: delete stale /etc/udev/rules.d/ files. Ubuntu: delete 70-persistent-net.rules, reset eth0.cfg to DHCP, clear /var/lib/dhcp/.

symptom: RDP connection to Azure Windows VM fails with error: The trust relationship between this workstation and the primary domain failed. Unable to log on with domain credentials.
rootCause: Active Directory Secure Channel between the VM and the primary domain controller is broken. The machine secret password is not synchronized with the domain controller.
solution: 1) Check connectivity to domain controller (set | find LOGONSERVER, Test-Connection). 2) Test secure channel: Test-ComputerSecureChannel -verbose. 3) Repair: Test-ComputerSecureChannel -Repair. 4) If repair fails, reset machine password: Reset-ComputerMachinePassword. 5) Last resort: Remove-Computer to unjoin domain, then Add-Computer to rejoin.

symptom: Boot Diagnostics screenshot in Azure portal appears stale (e.g. Windows login screen shows an old timestamp). The screenshot does not refresh to reflect the current VM state.
rootCause: The virtual display timeout (VIDEOIDLE) is enabled inside the guest OS, causing the display to power off after inactivity. For VMs created from a specialized VHD, the Azure provisioning agent does not run the powercfg command that disables this timeout. Older generalized-image VMs may also lack this setting.
solution: Disable the virtual display timeout in the guest OS. For Windows (elevated CMD): powercfg /setacvalueindex SCHEME_CURRENT SUB_VIDEO VIDEOIDLE 0. For Linux: xset s off. On Windows, the Azure provisioning agent normally sets this during first-boot from a generalized image (logged in C:\Windows\Panther\WaSetup.xml), but for specialized VHDs or older VMs the command must be run manually.

symptom: Cannot RDP to Azure Windows VM because the network interface (NIC) inside the guest OS is disabled or disconnected. No RDP or other network connectivity to the VM.
rootCause: The network interface in the VM guest OS has been disabled, either manually, by a script, or by a misconfiguration. The NIC shows as disconnected in Device Manager or Network Connections.
solution: Use Azure Serial Console to re-enable the NIC: connect to SAC, open CMD channel, run "netsh interface show interface" to identify the disabled NIC name, then run "netsh interface set interface name="<NIC-Name>" admin=enabled". If Serial Console is unavailable, use Azure portal or PowerShell to reset the network interface by changing the IP address to another available IP in the subnet.

symptom: Azure VM running Windows Server 2003 or Windows Server 2008/R2 - operating system has reached End of Support (EOS). No security updates, no technical support available for the OS.
rootCause: Windows Server 2008/R2 extended support ended January 14, 2020; Azure ESU support ended January 9, 2024. Windows Server 2003 extended support ended July 14, 2015. These OS versions no longer receive security or software updates.
solution: Migrate workloads to a newer Windows Server version on Azure. Use Azure Marketplace pre-configured images. For Windows Server 2003, limited Azure support is available but only for non-OS-level issues. Use Windows Server 2012 migration strategy or newer as target.

symptom: Azure Windows VM fails to boot or displays BSOD due to corrupted or missing system binary file (.sys). Error message in boot diagnostics indicates a required file is missing or contains errors.
rootCause: System binary file in \Windows\System32\Drivers is corrupted or missing, preventing Windows from loading the required driver. Could be caused by failed update, disk corruption, or manual file modification.
solution: Offline repair: 1) Attach OS disk to repair VM. 2) Run chkdsk <drive>: /F on the disk. 3) If corrupt binary found, rename to .old. 4) Search \Windows\WinSxS for the latest version using dir <binary-name> /s. 5) Copy latest version to \Windows\System32\Drivers\. 6) If no working copy in WinSxS, copy from another working VM with same OS and update level. 7) Detach disk and recreate VM.

symptom: Cannot add a VM to an existing availability set. Azure returns allocation failure because the VM series/size is not compatible with VMs already in the availability set.
rootCause: Azure availability sets have hardware constraints. Only certain VM series can be mixed within the same set (e.g., B, D v2-v5 are cross-compatible). Other series require specific hardware and cannot coexist in the same availability set.
solution: Use the Availability Sets REST API (List Available Sizes) to check which VM sizes are supported for the existing availability set before adding VMs. If needed, create a new availability set or migrate existing VMs to a compatible series. Reference the compatibility matrix: B/D-v2/D-v3/D-v4/D-v5 series are mutually compatible.

symptom: Azure Windows VM BSOD with stop code DRIVER_IRQL_NOT_LESS_OR_EQUAL (0x000000D1). VM cannot boot, RDP inaccessible. Boot diagnostics shows blue screen. May occur after installing July 2018 Windows KB updates.
rootCause: Known race condition bug in specific July 2018 Windows updates (KB4338819/KB4338825/KB4338814/KB4338824/KB4338830/KB4338820/KB4338823/KB4338818) causes driver crash on multiple Windows versions. If not update-related, a driver is accessing invalid memory at incorrect IRQL.
solution: 1) If caused by KB update: offline attach OS disk to repair VM, use DISM to remove problematic KB: dism /Image:<drive>:\ /Remove-Package /PackageName:<package>. Apply fix KBs listed per OS version. 2) If not KB-related: collect memory dump from \Windows\Memory.dmp on repair VM, submit support ticket with dump file for analysis. 3) Reassemble VM using az vm repair.

symptom: GPU functionality disabled on Azure N-Series VMs running Ubuntu 16.04 after upgrading to kernel 4.4.0-75. Kernel NULL pointer dereference in nvidia module logged in serial output.
rootCause: Bug in kernel version 4.4.0-75 causing NVIDIA driver crash when initializing GPU adapter.
solution: Upgrade the kernel to at least version 4.4.0-77. The Marketplace image for Ubuntu 16.04 LTS with the 4.4.0-77 kernel is published and available.

symptom: Linux VM fails to boot with kernel panic: VFS: Unable to mount root fs on unknown-block(0,0) or boot error file /initramfs-*.img not found after a kernel update.
rootCause: Missing initramfs image file for the updated kernel. The initramfs was not generated during the patching process, or the GRUB config has the initrd entry missing.
solution: Regenerate initramfs: RHEL/CentOS use dracut -f /boot/initramfs-{version}.img {version}; SLES use dracut -f /boot/initrd-{version} {version}; Ubuntu use mkinitramfs -k -o /boot/initrd.img-{version}. Alternatively use ALAR scripts: az vm repair repair-button --button-command initrd. Boot from older kernel via Serial Console GRUB menu as temporary workaround.

symptom: Linux VM fails to boot with kernel panic: Attempted to kill init! with dracut warning Boot has failed.
rootCause: Multiple possible causes: missing system files/directories, corrupted core libraries (e.g., libc.so), wrong file permissions (chmod 777 on /), missing partitions, or SELinux permission issues.
solution: Attach OS disk to repair VM and use chroot. Missing packages (RPM): rpm -ivh --root=/rescue package.rpm --replacepkgs. Wrong permissions: rpm -a --setperms. SELinux: add selinux=0 to kernel cmdline, then touch /.autorelabel and reboot. If unrecoverable, restore VM from backup.

symptom: Azure Linux VMs running Azure Linux VM Agent 2.1.5 or 2.1.6 cannot process extensions (install, delete, update, get status). Abnormal high CPU during walinuxagent process.
rootCause: Bug in Azure Linux VM Agent 2.1.5/2.1.6 triggered when the agent receives more than one automatic update.
solution: Manually upgrade Azure Linux VM Agent to latest version. SLES: zypper up python-azure-agent && systemctl restart waagent.service. Ubuntu: apt install walinuxagent && systemctl restart walinuxagent. Temporary workaround: service walinuxagent restart.

symptom: Azure Linux Agent shows Not Ready status. Error in /var/log/waagent.log: An error occurred while retrieving the goal state. VM cannot communicate with Fabric Controller.
rootCause: VM cannot reach WireServer IP 168.63.129.16 due to local firewall (IPTables), NSG, or third-party firewall blocking ports 80, 443, 32526.
solution: Allow outbound communication to 168.63.129.16 on ports 80, 443, 32526. Test: curl http://168.63.129.16/?comp=versions. Check IPTables and third-party firewall. Verify AutoUpdate.Enabled=y in /etc/waagent.conf. If service stopped, start it.

symptom: Azure VM agent or extensions not supported; support requests may be denied or agent/extensions malfunction. Linux Agent version below 2.2.53.1 or Windows Agent below 2.7.41491.1010.
rootCause: VM Guest Agent version is below the minimum supported version. After July 2022, Linux Agent versions below 2.2.53.1 are unsupported for published images. Windows Agent versions below 2.7.41491.1010 are unsupported. 32-bit OS does not support VM Guest Agent at all.
solution: Linux: update via distribution package manager and enable auto-update (waagent --version to check). If distro repos lack min version but agent is >2.1.7, enable auto-update feature. Windows: update per docs. For Linux, check version with waagent --version; Goal State Agent version is the effective version for extension handling.

symptom: Azure VM boot diagnostics shows "This is not a bootable disk. Please insert a bootable floppy and press any key to try again." VM cannot start.
rootCause: OS boot process cannot locate an active system partition. Possible missing reference in Boot Configuration Data (BCD) store preventing it from finding the Windows partition. Partition may not be marked as Active.
solution: 1) Attach OS disk to recovery VM. 2) Use DISKPART to verify and set the system partition as Active (diskpart > sel disk > sel partition > active). 3) Run bcdboot <Windows partition>:\Windows /S <partition>: to recreate boot record. 4) Run CHKDSK /f on the disk to fix errors. 5) Enable Serial Console and memory dump collection. 6) Detach disk and rebuild original VM.

symptom: Windows VM Guest Agent shows Not Ready status. Extensions fail. WaAppAgent.log shows Unable to connect to remote server 168.63.129.16:32526 or Service Unavailable (503). Error GetVersions() failed.
rootCause: VM cannot reach WireServer IP 168.63.129.16 on ports 80 and 32526. Caused by: DHCP disabled and static IP misconfigured, Windows Firewall or third-party firewall blocking ports 80/32526, proxy intercepting traffic, or multiple private IPs with incorrect primary IP configuration.
solution: 1) Enable DHCP on guest NIC: netsh interface ip set address name="NIC" source=dhcp. 2) Verify firewall allows ports 80 and 32526 to 168.63.129.16. 3) For multi-IP VMs ensure primary IP in Windows matches Azure portal primary IP (Set-NetIPAddress SkipAsSource). 4) Check proxy settings. 5) Test connectivity: Test-NetConnection 168.63.129.16 -Port 80.

symptom: Windows VM Guest Agent cannot discover fabric address. WaAppAgent.log shows: Attempting to discover fabric address on interface Npcap Loopback Adapter, Empty DHCP option data returned, Did not discover fabric address on interface Npcap Loopback Adapter.
rootCause: Wireshark (Nmap Npcap) loopback adapter installed on the VM interferes with Guest Agent DHCP-based fabric address discovery. The agent tries to discover the fabric address on the Npcap adapter instead of the real NIC.
solution: Disable the Npcap Loopback Adapter in Windows Network Connections (ncpa.cpl). Right-click the adapter > Disable. Guest Agent will then discover fabric address on the correct NIC.

symptom: Azure VM extension fails with FailedToDecryptProtectedSettings, CryptographicException 'Keyset does not exist', or 'The enveloped-data message does not contain the specified recipient' — transport certificate for extension protected settings is missing or has incorrect ACL on Crypto\RSA\MachineKeys folder
rootCause: Azure platform change (circa May 2020) altered extension workflow and CRP certificate dependency; the Windows Azure CRP Certificate Generator certificate may be missing, expired, or have incorrect permissions on MachineKeys/SystemKeys folders
solution: 1) Delete the 'Windows Azure CRP Certificate Generator' certificate from LocalMachine\My store (VM will auto-regenerate it), then trigger a new goal state via Update-AzVM or VM Reapply; 2) If ACL issue: fix permissions on C:\ProgramData\Microsoft\Crypto\RSA\MachineKeys using icacls — grant SYSTEM:(F) and Administrators:(RX) to the cert's unique key container file; 3) If still failing, stop/deallocate then start the VM

symptom: Windows VM fails to boot with 'Boot failure. Reboot and Select proper Boot device or Insert Boot Media in selected Boot device' — boot partition cannot be found
rootCause: Three possible causes: 1) OS partition holding BCD Store is inactive (Gen1 VMs only); 2) BCD (Boot Configuration Data) corruption; 3) Boot sector not found
solution: Attach OS disk to repair VM. 1) For inactive partition: use diskpart to select the OS partition → run 'active' command; 2) For BCD corruption: run chkdsk /f, then use bcdedit to rebuild BCD entries — set bootmgr device, set Windows Boot Loader device/osdevice to correct partitions, enable integrityservices, set bootstatuspolicy IgnoreAllFailures; 3) Rebuild VM from repaired disk using VM Repair Commands step 5

symptom: Windows VM fails to boot with INACCESSIBLE_BOOT_DEVICE blue screen error or 'Boot failure. Reboot and Select proper Boot device' message
rootCause: BCD (Boot Configuration Data) is corrupted, or the partition containing the Windows installation is marked inactive
solution: First try stop/deallocate and start the VM. If persists: attach OS disk to repair VM → 1) Verify and set boot partition as active using diskpart (Gen1 only); 2) Run bcdboot to create boot record; 3) Repair BCD with bcdedit — set bootmgr device, set Windows Boot Loader device/osdevice/integrityservices/recoveryenabled Off/bootstatuspolicy IgnoreAllFailures; 4) For single-partition VHD try 'device boot' instead of partition letter; 5) Detach and create new VM from repaired disk

symptom: Windows activation fails on Azure VM — error codes include 0xC004F074 (No KMS could be contacted), 0xC004FD01/0xC004FD02 (not on supported Hyper-V platform), 0xC004F06C (request timestamp invalid), 0xC004E015, 0x800705B4, 0x80070005 (Access denied)
rootCause: VM cannot reach KMS endpoint for activation — possible causes include network routing issues (forced tunneling), incorrect KMS endpoint configuration, duplicate Client Machine ID, or platform virtualization detection failure
solution: 1) Run 'slmgr.vbs /ato' to identify error code; 2) Verify correct KMS endpoint: Azure Public = azkms.core.windows.net:1688, Azure China = kms.core.chinacloudapi.cn:1688 or azkms.core.chinacloudapi.cn:1688, Azure US Gov = kms.core.usgovcloudapi.net:1688; 3) For forced tunneling: configure custom routes to allow KMS traffic; 4) For specific error codes see dedicated troubleshooting articles per error code; 5) Use Azure VM Windows Activation Troubleshooting Tools (windows-activation-troubleshoot-tools)

symptom: Linux VM fails to boot, drops to dracut emergency shell with error: /dev/VG/SwapVol does not exist. GRUB config has rd.lvm.lv=VG/SwapVol for a non-existent swap logical volume.
rootCause: GRUB configuration file (/etc/default/grub) contains rd.lvm.lv parameter pointing to a swap LV that does not exist. The VM cannot locate the specified LV during boot and enters dracut emergency mode.
solution: Remove the rd.lvm.lv=VG/SwapVol parameter from /etc/default/grub GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX entry. Use serial console to edit GRUB at boot (Ctrl+X after editing linux line) or offline rescue VM with chroot. After removing parameter, regenerate GRUB config. Note: using swap device via rd.lvm.lv is not recommended for Azure Linux VMs; use swap file instead.

symptom: Linux VM fails to boot and enters dracut/initramfs emergency shell. Root file system cannot be mounted. initramfs or initrd image is corrupted or incomplete.
rootCause: The initrd/initramfs image has corruption which prevents mounting the root volume and starting the OS. Can be caused by interrupted kernel update, disk issues, or manual misconfiguration.
solution: Use offline rescue VM (az vm repair create) to mount the OS disk, chroot into the filesystem, and regenerate initramfs: For RHEL/SLES run dracut -f -v, for Ubuntu/Debian run mkinitramfs -k -o /boot/initrd.img-3.6.6-1cdd4371.x86_64. After regeneration, swap OS disk back with az vm repair restore.

symptom: Azure Windows VM stuck at boot showing "Checking file system" or "Scanning and repairing drive (C:)" - chkdsk.exe running during boot, VM not starting normally
rootCause: NTFS file system error detected, typically caused by unexpected VM restart or abrupt shutdown interrupting disk operations
solution: 1. If VM eventually boots, wait for chkdsk to complete. 2. If stuck: take OS disk snapshot → attach to repair VM → run "chkdsk E: /f" on attached disk → detach and re-attach as OS disk to original VM

symptom: Azure Windows VM OS fully loaded but cannot RDP; Network Store Interface (NSI) service not starting. Event logs show Event ID 7022/7001/7000 for NSI service hung/failed/dependency error. DHCP Client also fails due to NSI dependency.
rootCause: NSI service is disabled, hung/crashing, or a required dependency service is not running. NSI is critical for network stack - without it DHCP and other network services fail.
solution: Connect via Serial Console → run "sc query NSI": if stopped → "sc start NSI"; if starting/stopping → "sc stop NSI" then "sc start NSI"; if disabled → "sc config NSI start=auto" then "sc start NSI". If dependency fails, repeat process for the dependency service.

symptom: Azure Windows VM displays blue screen (BSOD) with bug check 0x00000019 BAD_POOL_HEADER visible in Boot Diagnostics screenshot
rootCause: Memory pool corruption - the pool was already corrupted when the current request was made. Exact source requires detailed dump analysis.
solution: Collect OS memory dump file using documented procedure, then create Azure support request for dump analysis. No self-service fix available - requires memory dump analysis by support.

symptom: Azure Windows VM fully loaded but RDP/network traffic blocked. Guest OS firewall is misconfigured blocking some or all network traffic.
rootCause: Guest operating system firewall rules misconfigured - may block RDP (TCP 3389), WinRM (HTTP), SMB, or ICMP traffic.
solution: Online: Connect via Serial Console → query firewall rules with "netsh advfirewall firewall show rule dir=in name=all" → enable disabled rules with "netsh advfirewall firewall set rule name=<RULE> new enable=yes" or temporarily disable all profiles with "netsh advfirewall set allprofiles state off". Offline: attach OS disk to repair VM → enable/disable firewall rules via registry.

symptom: Azure portal Boot Diagnostics blade shows error "Failed to get contents of the log" when viewing VM screenshot. Error includes RPC_Exception for getExtendedInfoBlob.
rootCause: Three possible causes: 1) VM runs Windows Server 2008 R2 or earlier (no VM Health Report support). 2) Old VM Guest Agent version (<2.7.1198.806). 3) VM Guest Agent fails to generate Health Report due to unresolved SIDs in Local Administrators group or domain policy issues.
solution: Workaround 1: Upgrade OS to newer version. Workaround 2: Update VM Guest Agent to version >=2.7.1198.822. Workaround 3: Remove unresolved SIDs from Local Administrators group, check domain policy. Workaround 4: Redeploy VM to different host (triggers restart).

symptom: Azure VM 启动时卡在 "Applying Group Policy Local Users and Groups policy" 或 "Please wait" 画面，Boot Diagnostics 可见该截图（仅影响 Windows Server 2012/2012 R2）
rootCause: 组策略 "Delete user profiles older than a specified number of days on system restart" (CleanupProfiles) 在清理旧用户配置文件时产生冲突锁，导致启动挂起
solution:
1. 通过 VM repair commands 创建修复 VM 并挂载 OS 盘
2. 在注册表编辑器中加载 BROKENSOFTWARE hive (\windows\system32\config\SOFTWARE)
3. 删除 CleanupProfiles 键: reg delete "HKLM\BROKENSOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\System" /v CleanupProfiles /f
4. 卸载 hive 并重建 VM
5. 永久方案：不要在 VM 上使用 CleanupProfiles 策略

symptom: RDP 连接 Azure Windows VM 时报错 "You must change your password before logging on the first time. Please update your password or contact your system administrator or technical support"
rootCause: 目标账户被标记为首次登录必须更改密码（password change on first login flag），RDP 无法在连接阶段完成密码更改
solution:
1. 确认 VM Agent 是否正常运行（Azure Portal → VM → Properties → Agent status）
2. Agent 正常：通过 Run Command 执行 net user <USERNAME> *（本地账户）或 net user <USERNAME> * /domain（域账户）设置新密码
3. Agent 不可用：使用 reset-local-password-without-agent 离线重置密码
4. 密码更改后即可正常 RDP 登录，无需重启

symptom: RDP 连接 Azure Windows VM 时报错 "Too many admin sessions open"，但使用 mstsc /admin 可以连接
rootCause: 已达到 RDP 最大并发连接数限制（MaxConnections 注册表值限制了 Terminal Server Gateway 连接数）
solution:
1. 通过 Azure Serial Console 或 Remote PowerShell 连接到 VM
2. 查询当前限制: reg query "HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\TerminalServerGateway\Config\Core" /v MaxConnections
3. 设置为无限制: reg add "HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\TerminalServerGateway\Config\Core" /v MaxConnections /t REG_DWORD /d 0xffffffff /f
4. 重启 VM 后重新连接

symptom: Azure VM running Windows Server 2012 R2 experiences poor performance; local resource VHD (temp disk) I/O severely degraded; VM storage stack is in emulated mode
rootCause: Bug in Windows exposed when provisioned image has phantom IDE devices; storflt driver gets uninstalled from VM storage device stack unexpectedly, causing VM to use slower emulated mode path on every restart
solution: Install update KB4057903 (Hyper-V integration components update) available through Windows Update to resolve the storflt driver uninstall issue

symptom: Azure Windows VM fails to boot with BSOD stop error 0x000000EF "Critical Process Died"; boot diagnostics screenshot shows "Your PC ran into a problem and needs to restart"
rootCause: A critical system process (csrss.exe, wininit.exe, etc.) fails during boot, typically due to OS file corruption
solution: 1) Create repair VM using VM Repair Commands; 2) Run SFC offline: sfc /scannow /offbootdir=<BOOT>:\ /offwindir=<OS>:\windows; 3) If SFC fails, collect memory dump (enable via registry CrashControl keys) and submit to Microsoft Support; 4) Rebuild VM

symptom: Azure Windows VM becomes unresponsive during restart; boot diagnostics shows OS stuck displaying "Default Domain Controllers Policy" message
rootCause: Recent changes to the Default Domain Controllers Policy cause the VM to hang during Group Policy application at boot
solution: 1) Undo recent Default Domain Controllers Policy changes; 2) If root cause unclear, attach OS disk to recovery VM, collect Memory.dmp from \Windows folder, submit to Microsoft Support; 3) If dump not found, use NMI calls via Serial Console to generate crash dump

symptom: Azure Windows VM fails to boot with error 0xc00000ba: "The operating system couldn't be loaded because a critical system driver is missing or contains errors"
rootCause: Windows system files are corrupt, possibly due to unfinished installation/erasure, bad application deletion, malware/Trojan attack, or improper shutdown
solution: 1) Attach OS disk to troubleshooting VM; 2) Try disabling recently installed service via registry (HKLM\ControlSet001\services\<ServiceName> Start=4); 3) Run DISM /cleanup-image /restorehealth using WinRE image or full ISO as source; 4) Run SFC /scannow offline; 5) Recreate VM

symptom: Azure Windows VM fails to boot with "BOOTMGR is missing. Press Ctrl+Alt+Del to restart" error message
rootCause: OS boot process cannot locate active system partition; missing reference in BCD store to locate Windows partition; possible ransomware attack encrypting boot files
solution: 1) Create repair VM; 2) Verify OS partition is active using DISKPART (list partition → detail partition → active); 3) Run bcdboot <WinPartition>:\Windows to recreate boot record; 4) Fix BCD store references using bcdedit commands for Gen1/Gen2 VMs; 5) Check for ransomware (.txt ransom notes in root folder); 6) Rebuild VM

symptom: Windows VM fails to boot with error 0xC0000225: a critical system driver (.sys file) is missing or contains errors. Boot diagnostics shows Windows Boot Manager error referencing a file in \Windows\System32\drivers\.
rootCause: A system binary (.sys) file in the drivers directory is missing or corrupted, preventing the OS from loading.
solution: Attach the OS disk to a repair VM. Run chkdsk on the disk. If the binary is corrupt, rename it to .old and copy the latest version from \Windows\WinSxS\ to \Windows\System32\Drivers\. Alternatively, restore from a VM backup.

symptom: Windows VM fails to boot with error 0xC0000225: "The boot selection failed because a required device is inaccessible." No file name is displayed in the error screen.
rootCause: The Boot Configuration Data (BCD) is corrupted, or the VHD was migrated from on-premises and prepared incorrectly.
solution: Attach the OS disk to a repair VM. Identify the Boot partition and Windows partition. Use bcdedit /enum to get the Windows Boot Loader identifier, then repair BCD with bcdedit /set commands to set OSDEVICE to the correct partition. Recreate the VM from the repaired disk.

symptom: Windows VM fails to boot with error 0xC0000225 referencing \WINDOWS\system32\config\system: "The operating system couldn't be loaded because the system registry file is missing or contains errors."
rootCause: The SYSTEM registry hive is corrupted, possibly due to hive failure, empty hive mount, or hive not closed correctly.
solution: Attach the OS disk to a repair VM. Back up \Windows\System32\config\. Open Registry Editor, load HKEY_USERS > Load Hive with the SYSTEM file. If it loads without issues, the hive was not closed correctly — unload it to fix. If loading fails with an error, contact Azure Support.

symptom: Azure VM created from NetApp Cloud Volumes ONTAP (CVO) image shows error: 'Cloud Volumes ONTAP is not compatible with Azure VM extensions. VM extensions are currently installed on your Azure VM. Contact Microsoft Azure support to remove those extensions.'
rootCause: CVO doesn't support Azure VM extensions because extensions affect BlueXP management operations. Although CVO VM appears as Linux (ONTAP version) in the Azure portal, it's actually a highly customized derivative of the FreeBSD operating system. Starting in BlueXP 3.9.54, NetApp enforces this limitation as a notification.
solution: Run PowerShell or CLI script to remove all extensions from the VM model (sets vm.resources to empty array via ARM API PUT). To prevent future extensions: either set Azure Policy to deny Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/extensions, or set AllowExtensionOperations to false on the VM's OSProfile. Note: remove CVO resource group 'delete' lock before running the script; operation may take 20-30 minutes if guest agent doesn't exist.

symptom: Cannot upgrade RHEL-SAP-HANA or RHEL-SAP-APPS pay-as-you-go (PAYG) Azure VM from RHEL 8.x to 8.10 using standard 'dnf update' command. Update fails or repos show errors because RHEL for SAP 8.10 content is available in unversioned repositories instead of E4S/EUS repos used by earlier 8.x versions.
rootCause: RHEL for SAP 8.10 follows a different repository approach from previous 8.x versions. While 8.x (x != 10) uses Extended Update Services for SAP Solutions (E4S) and EUS, 8.10 content is only available in unversioned repositories. The existing RHUI E4S/EUS packages and version lock files prevent access to the correct repos.
solution: 1) Remove existing RHUI package: dnf remove $(rpm -qa | grep -i rhui). 2) Remove version lock: rm /etc/yum/vars/releasever. 3) Create new repo config pointing to unversioned repos (rhel8-base-sap-ha for SAP-HANA, rhel8-base-sap-apps for SAP-APPS). 4) Install new RHUI package (rhui-azure-rhel8-base-sap-ha or rhui-azure-rhel8-base-sap-apps). 5) Verify repos with dnf repolist. 6) Run dnf update and reboot. Note: RHEL 8.10 is the final RHEL 8 release under Maintenance Support 2 Phase.

symptom: Azure Windows VM BSOD with stop error DRIVER_IRQL_NOT_LESS_OR_EQUAL (0x000000D1) referencing mlx5.sys driver module. VM fails and restarts unexpectedly or becomes unstable. Dump file analysis shows faulting module mlx5.sys.
rootCause: Outdated NVIDIA Mellanox mlx5 network adapter driver in the guest VM. The mlx5.sys driver is used on VM SKUs with Accelerated Networking (Mellanox ConnectX adapters). When the installed driver version is incompatible with host firmware or platform updates, it triggers a kernel memory access violation.
solution: 1) Check for Mellanox adapter: Get-PnpDevice -Class Net | Where-Object { $_.FriendlyName -match "Mellanox|ConnectX|mlx5" }. 2) Check driver version: Get-WmiObject Win32_PnPSignedDriver | Where-Object { $_.DeviceName -match "Mellanox" }. 3) Update driver via Windows Update (recommended), Device Manager, or download from NVIDIA Mellanox portal. 4) Run Azure VM Mellanox Driver Validation Tool to verify fix. Affected: VMs with Accelerated Networking (HB, HC, ND, NDv2, HBv3, N-series).

symptom: Azure VM creation, start, or resize fails with error code AllocationFailed or ZonalAllocationFailed: "Allocation failed. We do not have sufficient capacity for the requested VM size in this region." Standalone VM not part of availability set or proximity placement group.
rootCause: Azure lacks sufficient compute capacity for the requested VM size in the specified region or availability zone. Unprecedented growth in demand for Azure services in specific regions can cause resource allocation failures.
solution: 1) Retry the allocation after a short period (may be temporary). 2) Resize to a different VM size with more availability. 3) Deploy in a different region or availability zone. 4) Consider On-demand Capacity Reservations for guaranteed capacity. Portal may suggest alternative VM sizes or zones.

symptom: Azure VM deployment fails with OverconstrainedZonalAllocationRequest or OverconstrainedAllocationRequest. Multiple deployment constraints (VM size, Accelerated Networking, Availability Zone, Ephemeral disk, Proximity Placement Group, Ultra/PremiumSSDv2 disk) cannot all be satisfied simultaneously.
rootCause: Azure Compute platform cannot allocate a VM to meet all required constraints. The combination of VM size/SKU + Accelerated Networking + Availability Zone + Ephemeral disk + PPG + Ultra/PremiumSSDv2 disk narrows available resource pool too much.
solution: 1) Retry allocation later. 2) Resize VM to different size. 3) Deploy in different region/zone or remove zonal constraint. 4) Reduce constraints: disable accelerated networking, remove from PPG, remove Ultra/PremiumSSDv2 disks. 5) Create new VM from OS disk snapshot in a different zone.

symptom: Allocation failure for older/legacy VM sizes (Av1, Dv1, DSv1, D15v2, DS15v2 series). VM creation or start fails because older series VMs do not run on latest generation Azure infrastructure.
rootCause: As Azure infrastructure expands, newer-generation hardware is deployed for latest VM types. Some older series VMs (Av1, Dv1, DSv1, D15v2) do not run on latest generation infrastructure, causing occasional allocation failures for these legacy SKUs.
solution: Migrate to equivalent newer VM series: Av1→Av2, Dv1/DSv1 (D1-D5)→Dv3/DSv3, Dv1/DSv1 (D11-D14)→Ev3/ESv3, D15v2/DS15v2→D16v3/DS16v3 or D32v3/DS32v3 or E64i_v3/E64is_v3 (isolated). These newer VMs are optimized for latest hardware with better pricing and performance.

symptom: Windows 11 deployment or upgrade fails on Azure VM. VM SKU does not support Windows 11. Azure portal may allow selecting Win11 image but deployment on unsupported VM SKUs (A-series, Av2, B-series, Dv2, Dv3, Ev3, F-series, G/GS-series, L-series, Lsv2, M-series, NC/NCv2/NCv3, ND, NV/NVv2/NVv3) results in compatibility issues.
rootCause: Windows 11 requires Intel 8th gen+ or AMD Zen 2+ CPUs. Older Azure VM SKUs run on Haswell (Intel 4th gen), Broadwell (5th gen), or AMD Naples (Zen 1) processors that do not meet Windows 11 CPU generation requirements. The Azure portal may still allow selecting these VM sizes for Win11 images.
solution: Use a Windows 11 compatible VM SKU: Dv4/Dv5, Bsv2, Fsv2, Eav4/Ev4/Ev5, Lsv3, Msv2/Mv2, NCasT4_v3, NVv4, NVadsA10_v5, or newer series. Check the Windows 11 support matrix at the source URL. VMs must also be Generation 2 with Trusted Launch, Secure Boot, and vTPM enabled.

symptom: Cannot upgrade Azure VM to Windows 11 version 22H2 or later. Upgrade blocked or fails with compatibility check errors. PC Health Check app reports VM does not meet requirements.
rootCause: Windows 11 on Azure VMs requires: (1) Generation 2 VM, (2) Trusted Launch enabled with Secure Boot and vTPM, (3) compatible CPU. VMs deployed before June 28, 2023 defaulted to standard security (no Trusted Launch). Gen1 VMs and VMs without Trusted Launch cannot run Windows 11 22H2+. Trusted Launch can only be set during VM creation (prior to upgrade support).
solution: 1) Upgrade from Gen1 to Gen2 by upgrading to Trusted Launch security type (supported path). 2) For existing Gen2 VMs without Trusted Launch, upgrade security type to Trusted Launch. 3) Ensure VM SKU supports Windows 11 (Dv4+ or equivalent). 4) After enabling Trusted Launch with vTPM and Secure Boot, retry Windows 11 upgrade. Reference: Upgrading VMs from standard security to Trusted Launch documentation.

symptom: Certain Windows Server roles or features do not work or are not supported on Azure VMs. Customer deploys DHCP Server, Windows Deployment Services (WDS), Rights Management Services, RRAS/DirectAccess, Network Load Balancing (NLB), or attempts BitLocker on OS disk.
rootCause: Azure networking and storage architecture does not support certain Windows Server roles and features. DHCP Server is not supported on NICs connected to Azure VNet (only on internal networks in nested virtualization). BitLocker Drive Encryption is not supported on the OS hard disk (only data disks). Other unsupported features: NLB, RRAS, DirectAccess, SNMP, WINS, Wireless LAN, Multipath I/O, iSNS, Storage Manager for SANs.
