solution: Do not deploy unsupported roles/features on Azure VMs. Alternatives: For load balancing use Azure Load Balancer or Application Gateway instead of NLB. For VPN/remote access use Azure VPN Gateway instead of RRAS. For DHCP use Azure-provided DHCP. For disk encryption use Azure Disk Encryption (ADE) on data disks. For BitLocker on OS disk, use ADE which manages BitLocker via Key Vault. Hyper-V role is only supported on Ev3 and Dv3 series VMs.

symptom: Azure VM running Windows 10 — operating system reached End of Support (EOS). Concerns about security updates, compliance requirements, and continued support.
rootCause: Windows 10 reached End of Support. Computers running EOS operating systems no longer receive standard Microsoft support or security updates. Many compliance frameworks require running a currently supported OS.
solution: For Azure VMs: Extended Security Updates (ESU) for Windows 10 are automatically included at no additional cost. No MAK keys or manual activation required. ESU covers critical and important security updates only (no new features or non-security fixes). Options: (1) Continue with ESU on Azure (automatic). (2) Upgrade to Windows 11 on a compatible VM SKU (Gen2 + Trusted Launch required). Reference ESU program documentation for coverage details.

symptom: Need to assess whether a Windows VM in Azure is ready for an in-place OS upgrade; unclear if upgrade path is supported or if prerequisites (disk space, Trusted Launch, Secure Boot, vTPM) are met.
rootCause: Windows Server/Client in-place upgrades require specific OS version compatibility, minimum 64GB disk space, 4GB RAM, and proper Azure security feature configuration (Trusted Launch, Secure Boot, vTPM). Missing prerequisites cause upgrade failures.
solution: Run the Azure VM Windows OS Upgrade Assessment Tool (PowerShell script) to evaluate OS version, supported upgrade paths, disk space, memory, and Azure security features. Can be run via GitHub download or prepackaged Run Command. Follow remediation suggestions before attempting upgrade.

symptom: Azure VM is running a Windows Server version that has reached End of Support (EOS), such as Windows Server 2003 or Windows Server 2008/R2. Customer concerned about security updates and compliance.
rootCause: Microsoft ended extended support for Windows Server 2008/R2 on January 14, 2020 (Azure ESU ended January 9, 2024) and Windows Server 2003 on July 14, 2015. These versions no longer receive security updates.
solution: Migrate workloads to a newer Windows Server version on Azure. Pre-configured images with Windows and SQL Server are available in Azure Marketplace. For Windows Server 2003, the VM can still run in Azure but support is limited to Azure-level issues only (no OS-level troubleshooting).

symptom: Windows Updates fail to install on Azure VM; need to identify specific Windows servicing errors from CBS logs that indicate the need for an in-place upgrade or repair.
rootCause: Component-Based Servicing (CBS) logs may contain known error codes indicating corruption or issues in the Windows servicing stack that prevent updates from installing. These errors are categorized by severity (Critical/High/Medium/Low).
solution: Run the Azure VM Windows Update Error Detection Tool (PowerShell script) which scans CBS logs and archived .zip logs in C:\Windows\Logs\CBS, filters by configurable date range (default: last 30 days), categorizes errors by severity, and provides remediation guidance links. Can be run via GitHub download or prepackaged Run Command.

symptom: Windows Updates fail to install on Azure VM despite no specific error code; Windows Update servicing stack may be corrupted or in a broken state.
rootCause: The Windows Update servicing stack (including wuauserv, cryptsvc, BITS services, SoftwareDistribution and catroot2 folders) can become corrupted, preventing updates from installing.
solution: Run the Azure VM Windows Update Reset Tool (PowerShell script) which: 1) Stops wuauserv, cryptsvc, and BITS services, 2) Renames SoftwareDistribution and catroot2 folders with timestamped backups, 3) Re-registers core update-related DLLs, 4) Restarts services. Then retry the failed Windows Update. Can be run via GitHub download or prepackaged Run Command.

symptom: Cannot reset local Windows password on Azure VM because Azure guest agent is not installed or not functioning. Portal/PowerShell password reset (VMAccess extension) fails.
rootCause: The Azure guest agent (VMAccessAgent) is required for portal and PowerShell password reset operations. If the agent is not installed (e.g., custom uploaded image) or is malfunctioning, these methods are unavailable.
solution: Offline password reset via OS disk swap: 1) Stop affected VM and snapshot OS disk. 2) Create disk copy from snapshot, attach to a repair VM. 3) On repair VM, create Group Policy startup script: create gpt.ini in Windows/System32/GroupPolicy, scripts.ini in Machine/Scripts/, and FixAzureVM.cmd in Machine/Scripts/Startup/ containing "net user <username> <newpassword> /add /Y" + add to administrators and remote desktop users groups. 4) Detach disk and swap OS disk on original VM. 5) Boot VM, RDP with new credentials. 6) Clean up the GP script files.

symptom: RDP connection fails after entering credentials with error: This computer cannot connect to the remote computer. System Event Log shows Event ID 1058 or 1057 from TerminalServices-RemoteConnectionManager: RD Session Host Server has failed to replace/create the expired self-signed certificate used for TLS connections. Schannel Event 36870/36872 may also appear: fatal error accessing TLS server credential private key, error code 0x8009030D.
rootCause: Local RSA encryption keys in C:\ProgramData\Microsoft\Crypto\RSA\MachineKeys folder cannot be accessed due to incorrect NTFS permissions on the MachineKeys folder or individual RSA key files, or the RSA key is corrupted/missing.
solution: Fix MachineKeys permissions: run takeown /f MachineKeys /a /r then icacls to grant SYSTEM:(F), NETWORK SERVICE:(R), Administrators:(F). Restart TermService. If certificate still invalid, renew RDP self-signed cert via PowerShell: Remove-Item on Remote Desktop cert store, then restart SessionEnv service. If neither works, delete certificate from Remote Desktop store via MMC on another VM in the same VNET, then restart SessionEnv.
